source_paper_title: Rates of Convergence in the Central Limit Theorem for Markov Chains, with an Application to TD Learning
context:
Section 5 (Conclusions), page 19

Under some conditions, the rate of convergence in the central limit theorem for i.i.d. random vectors is O(1/\sqrt{n}) (Fang et al. 2019). It would be interesting to see if the O(\log n/\sqrt{n}) rate of convergence in Theorem 1 can be improved to O(1/\sqrt{n}) under possibly additional conditions than the ones used in this paper. The Edgeworth expansion for scalar functions of Markov chains in (Kontoyiannis and Meyn 2003) suggests that the log n could possibly be eliminated for Markov chains as well.
title: Eliminate the \(\log n\) factor in the Wasserstein rate for the martingale CLT (improve to \(O(1/\sqrt{n})\))
background_latex: Let \(\{m_k\}_{k\ge 1}\) be a \(d\)-dimensional martingale difference sequence with respect to a filtration \(\{\mathcal F_k\}_{k\ge 0}\), i.e., \(m_k\) is \(\mathcal F_k\)-adapted, \(\mathbb E(m_k\mid \mathcal F_{k-1})=0\), and \(\mathbb E\|m_k\|<\infty\). Fix \(\beta\in(0,1)\) and assume \(\mathbb E\|m_k\|^{2+\beta}<\infty\) for all \(k\). Define the partial sums \(S_n=\sum_{k=1}^n m_k\) and the normalized sum \(W_n=S_n/\sqrt{n}\). Let \(\Sigma_k:=\mathbb E(m_k m_k^\top\mid \mathcal F_{k-1})\) be the conditional covariance, and let \(\Sigma_\infty\succ 0\) be a target (asymptotic) covariance matrix. Let \(Z\sim \mathcal N(0,I_d)\). The \(1\)-Wasserstein distance is \[d_W(X,Y):=\sup_{h\in\mathrm{Lip}_1} \mathbb E[h(X)-h(Y)],\] where \(\mathrm{Lip}_1\) is the set of functions \(h:\mathbb R^d\to\mathbb R\) with Lipschitz constant at most \(1\). Theorem 1 in the paper gives a general \(d_W\) bound for \(W_n\) versus \(\Sigma_\infty^{1/2}Z\), and, for bounded martingale increments arising from finite-state Markov chains, yields a rate of order \(O(\log n/\sqrt{n})\) (via taking \(\beta=1-2/\log n\)).
problem_definition_latex:
Problem: Show that under suitable additional assumptions on \(\{m_k\}\) (beyond \(\mathbb E\|m_k\|^{2+\beta}<\infty\) and existence of \(\Sigma_k\), and with \(\Sigma_k\to \Sigma_\infty\) in an appropriate sense), the Wasserstein distance satisfies the sharper rate
\[d_W\bigl(W_n,\,\Sigma_\infty^{1/2}Z\bigr)=O\Bigl(\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\Bigr)\quad\text{as }n\to\infty,\]
thereby eliminating the \(\log n\) factor present in the paper's \(O(\log n/\sqrt{n})\) bound.
latex:
Let \(\{m_k\}_{k\ge 1}\) be a \(d\)-dimensional martingale difference sequence with respect to a filtration \(\{\mathcal F_k\}_{k\ge 0}\), i.e., \(m_k\) is \(\mathcal F_k\)-adapted, \(\mathbb E(m_k\mid \mathcal F_{k-1})=0\), and \(\mathbb E\|m_k\|<\infty\). Fix \(\beta\in(0,1)\) and assume \(\mathbb E\|m_k\|^{2+\beta}<\infty\) for all \(k\). Define the partial sums \(S_n=\sum_{k=1}^n m_k\) and the normalized sum \(W_n=S_n/\sqrt{n}\). Let \(\Sigma_k:=\mathbb E(m_k m_k^\top\mid \mathcal F_{k-1})\) be the conditional covariance, and let \(\Sigma_\infty\succ 0\) be a target (asymptotic) covariance matrix. Let \(Z\sim \mathcal N(0,I_d)\). The \(1\)-Wasserstein distance is \[d_W(X,Y):=\sup_{h\in\mathrm{Lip}_1} \mathbb E[h(X)-h(Y)],\] where \(\mathrm{Lip}_1\) is the set of functions \(h:\mathbb R^d\to\mathbb R\) with Lipschitz constant at most \(1\). Theorem 1 in the paper gives a general \(d_W\) bound for \(W_n\) versus \(\Sigma_\infty^{1/2}Z\), and, for bounded martingale increments arising from finite-state Markov chains, yields a rate of order \(O(\log n/\sqrt{n})\) (via taking \(\beta=1-2/\log n\)).

Problem:
Problem: Show that under suitable additional assumptions on \(\{m_k\}\) (beyond \(\mathbb E\|m_k\|^{2+\beta}<\infty\) and existence of \(\Sigma_k\), and with \(\Sigma_k\to \Sigma_\infty\) in an appropriate sense), the Wasserstein distance satisfies the sharper rate
\[d_W\bigl(W_n,\,\Sigma_\infty^{1/2}Z\bigr)=O\Bigl(\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\Bigr)\quad\text{as }n\to\infty,\]
thereby eliminating the \(\log n\) factor present in the paper's \(O(\log n/\sqrt{n})\) bound.
